cilantro.descFull: The leaf-half of the coriander plant — citrusy and bright where the seed is sweet and warm. Loved across the world's salsas and curries; despised by 4-14 percent of humans whose OR6A2 gene reads it as soap. The polarizing herb. The cooling herb.
cilantro.historicUses: Aztec sacred bath-herb. Mexican salsa essential. Indian chutney. Thai larb. Vietnamese pho garnish.
cilantro.compounds: Aldehydes (E-2-decenal, E-2-dodecenal), linalool, vitamin K, beta-carotene. Heavy-metal chelation (preliminary). Antimicrobial.
cilantro.cautions: Generally safe. Rare cross-allergy with parsley family. Genetic perception of soapiness in OR6A2 carriers.

spearmint.desc: The garden mint. Gentler than peppermint. Pressed into mojitos, tea, lamb sauce.
spearmint.descFull: The sweeter cousin to peppermint, lower in menthol, brighter in flavor. Sacred to Hades (Minthe, the nymph he loved). Pressed into Maghreb tea, Moroccan mechoui, English lamb sauce, mojitos. The hospitality herb. Spreader.
spearmint.historicUses: Greek funerary herb. Moroccan ceremonial tea. Mojito and julep essential. Yogurt-sauce mint.
spearmint.compounds: Carvone, limonene, menthone, menthol (much lower than peppermint). Digestive. Carminative. Mild antimicrobial.
spearmint.cautions: Generally very safe. May relax LES; some reflux caution. Allergy possible (Lamiaceae).

aleppo pepper.desc: Halaby pepper of Syria. Sun-dried crimson flakes. Smoky-sweet, moderate heat.
aleppo pepper.descFull: A coarsely ground pepper of northern Syria, sun-dried and salt-cured to crimson flakes. Smoky-sweet rather than searing. Now sourced largely from Turkey since the Syrian civil war. The flake of Levantine hospitality — sprinkled on hummus, kebab, lentil soup.
aleppo pepper.historicUses: Levantine staple since antiquity. Modern crisis: Syrian production disrupted; Turkish cultivation maintains the tradition.
aleppo pepper.compounds: Capsaicin (moderate), capsanthin, carotenoids, vitamin C. Anti-inflammatory. Vasodilator.
aleppo pepper.cautions: Mild-moderate heat. Avoid with reflux in large amounts. Skin/eye contact during handling.

habanero.props: Searing Fire · Banishment · Speed · Tropical Sun
habanero.desc: The lantern of the Yucatan. 100,000-350,000 SHU. Fruity-hot, citrus-bright, slow-burn.
habanero.descFull: A small lantern-shaped pepper of the Yucatan and Caribbean, ranging from orange to red. Hot — 100K to 350K Scoville. Fruity-citrus aroma underlying capsaicin fire. Heart of Yucatan salsa habanera, Caribbean jerk, hot-sauce fame.
habanero.historicUses: Pre-Columbian Mayan staple. Yucatan salsa habanera. Caribbean jerk. Modern: hot-sauce industry foundation.
habanero.compounds: Capsaicin, dihydrocapsaicin, citrus terpenes (limonene), capsanthin. Vanilloid receptor agonist. Endorphin-triggering.
habanero.cautions: Severe heat. Skin/eye contact requires gloves. GI distress possible. Avoid with reflux or ulcers.

jalapeño.props: Garden Fire · Festivity · Hospitality · Warm Earth
jalapeño.desc: The Mexican garden pepper. Moderate heat (2-8K SHU). Green-fresh, smoke-able into chipotle.
jalapeño.descFull: A thumb-sized green Mexican pepper, name of the town of Xalapa. Moderate heat (2,500-8,000 SHU). Used fresh in salsas and pickles, smoke-dried for chipotle. The friendly chile of taquerias, hot wings, and stuffed poppers.
jalapeño.historicUses: Nahuatl 'jalapeño' (of Xalapa). Pre-Columbian fresh chile. Smoked into chipotle (long tradition). American hot-pepper-rings ubiquity.
jalapeño.compounds: Capsaicin (moderate), capsanthin, vitamin C, vitamin A. Anti-inflammatory. Vasodilator.
jalapeño.cautions: Moderate heat. Capsaicin residue on hands transfers to eyes/face for hours. Wash with milk or oil, not water.

serrano chile.props: Mountain Fire · Crisp Heat · Bright Salsa · Sierra-Magic
serrano chile.desc: The Mexican mountain pepper. Hotter than jalapeño (10-25K SHU). Crisp, grassy, bright.
serrano chile.descFull: A thin Mexican pepper of the sierras, named for the mountains (serrano). Hotter than jalapeño, crisper, grassier. Pico de gallo essential. Eaten green most often, though red-ripe and dried versions exist. The Mexican home-cook's daily chile.
serrano chile.historicUses: Sierra Madre Oriental traditional fresh chile. Pico de gallo. Salsa verde. Often eaten raw with carnitas or carne asada.
serrano chile.compounds: Capsaicin (moderate-high), capsanthin, vitamin C. Vanilloid receptor agonist. Anti-inflammatory.
serrano chile.cautions: Higher heat than jalapeño. Gloves recommended when seeding in volume. Eye contact harsh.

thai chili.props: Searing Fire · Bird's-Eye Vision · Tropical Heat · Sharp Speed
thai chili.desc: The bird's-eye pepper. Small and savage (50-100K SHU). Heart of Thai, Vietnamese, Indonesian cooking.
thai chili.descFull: A small, narrow, red or green Southeast Asian pepper — phrik kee noo, prik chee fa. Birds eat them and spread the seeds (the bird's-eye name). Heart of Thai larb, Indonesian sambal, Vietnamese phở condiment. Sharp, clean, swift heat.
thai chili.historicUses: Pre-1500 Andean origin; Portuguese took to Southeast Asia where it became staple. Sambal, nuoc cham, larb.
thai chili.compounds: Capsaicin (high), dihydrocapsaicin, capsanthin, vitamin C. Strong vanilloid receptor activation. Thermogenic.
thai chili.cautions: High heat. Skin/eye contact severe. GI distress possible in large amounts. Wash hands well.

garlic.desc: The hearth-bulb. Ward against ill spirits, infection, and creeping cold. Backbone of cuisine.
garlic.descFull: The hard white bulb of a Eurasian allium, cultivated for 5000+ years. Egyptian pyramid-builders ate it for strength. Hung at thresholds against evil spirits and vampires. Pressed into mojo de ajo, aioli, allioli. The sharpest love.
garlic.historicUses: Egyptian builder's tonic. Greek athlete strength-food. European vampire-ward. Modern: cardiovascular and antimicrobial support.
garlic.compounds: Allicin (forms on crushing), diallyl disulfide, ajoene, vitamin C, selenium. Antimicrobial. Cardiovascular protective. Anti-platelet.
garlic.cautions: Anti-platelet; caution with anticoagulants or before surgery. GI irritation in large doses. Topical pure allicin burns skin.

black garlic.desc: Garlic transformed by slow heat. Forty days in low humidity turns it black, sweet, and balsamic.
black garlic.descFull: Whole garlic bulbs aged 40-60 days at low temperature in controlled humidity. Maillard browning converts allicin's pungency into deep umami sweetness — balsamic, fig, prune, soy. A Korean and Japanese tradition lately discovered by the West. The patient bulb.
black garlic.historicUses: Korean and Japanese fermented food tradition (decades). Western culinary fashion since ~2010. Garnish, paste, base.
black garlic.compounds: S-allylcysteine (water-soluble), reduced allicin, Maillard reaction products, antioxidants. Higher antioxidant than raw garlic.
black garlic.cautions: Generally very safe. Lower platelet effect than raw garlic. Sweetness deceives; still allium for sensitive stomachs.

onion.desc: The foundation bulb. Heart of every soup, every stew, every sauté. The grandmother of cuisine.
onion.descFull: The papery-skinned, fleshy-layered bulb of an allium. Every world's cuisine starts with the onion in the pan. Egyptians swore oaths upon it (its layers stood for eternity). Bishop Albertus called it the universal stomach-tonic. Layered, tearful, foundational.
onion.historicUses: Egyptian sacred bulb. Roman stomach-tonic. Mirepoix / sofrito / soffritto base. French onion soup. Indian masala-base.
onion.compounds: Quercetin, allyl sulfides, prebiotic fructans, vitamin C. Anti-inflammatory. Cardiovascular protective. Prebiotic.
onion.cautions: Generally safe. FODMAP-sensitive individuals may struggle. Toxic to dogs and cats; never feed pets.

shallot.props: Refinement · French Magic · Subtlety · Pearl Allium
shallot.desc: The clustered allium of French refinement. Subtler than onion, sweeter than garlic. The sauce-bulb.
shallot.descFull: The clustered cousin of the onion, multiple cloves to a bulb. Native to Central Asia, cultivated worldwide. Backbone of French vinaigrettes, béarnaise, mignonette. Sweeter, subtler than onion. Less fierce than garlic. The chef's secret bulb.
shallot.historicUses: Crusader-era introduction to Europe from Ascalon (city in Levant). French haute cuisine essential. Persian and Indian also.
shallot.compounds: Quercetin (higher than onion), allyl sulfides, vitamin C, organosulfur compounds. Cardiovascular. Mild antimicrobial.
shallot.cautions: Same allium cautions as onion. FODMAP-sensitive caution. Pet toxicity (Allium genus).

lemon peel.desc: The dried zest of the lemon. Sun-bright, concentrated, essential. The brightness of a dish in pinch-form.
lemon peel.descFull: The aromatic zest of the lemon, dried to amber-yellow shreds. Concentrated essence of citrus, no bitter, no juice. Pressed into Moroccan tagine spice mix (preserved lemon's cousin), Italian biscotti, Greek pastitsio, gingerbread, mulling spice. The bright pinch.
lemon peel.historicUses: Medieval European confectionery. Preserved-lemon adjacent. Modern: candle-making, baking, bitters.
lemon peel.compounds: d-Limonene (90%+), citral, vitamin C, flavonoids (hesperidin, eriocitrin), pectin. Antioxidant. Mood-elevating aroma.
lemon peel.cautions: Generally very safe. Photo-toxic in concentrated oil if applied topically and sun-exposed.

orange peel.desc: The dried zest of the orange. Warm, sweet, faintly bitter. Yule's perfume, biscotti's depth.
orange peel.descFull: The aromatic zest of the sweet orange, dried to amber strips. Warmer and sweeter than lemon peel, with a hint of bitter to deepen baking and braising. Pressed into Yule pomanders studded with clove, into duck à l'orange, into biscotti, into mulling spice.
orange peel.historicUses: Yule pomander essential. Renaissance perfume base. Chinese medicine (chen pi, aged). Mulling spice classic.
orange peel.compounds: d-Limonene, beta-myrcene, alpha-pinene, hesperidin, vitamin C. Antioxidant. Mood-elevating aroma.
orange peel.cautions: Generally safe. Photo-toxic concentrated oils with sun exposure. Pesticide residue concern on commercial peels.

lime peel.props: Tropical Brightness · Cleansing · Sea-Breeze · Sharp Magic
lime peel.desc: The dried zest of the lime. Sharper and greener than lemon. Soul of mojito, ceviche, key lime.
lime peel.descFull: The aromatic zest of the lime, dried to pale green-amber. Sharper than lemon, more floral, with a tropical edge. Pressed into Caribbean jerk, Mexican carne asada, Thai green curry, mojito, key lime pie. The bright sharpness.
lime peel.historicUses: British Navy scurvy-prevention (limeys). Caribbean and Latin American kitchen. Modern bartending essential.
lime peel.compounds: d-Limonene, beta-pinene, gamma-terpinene, vitamin C, flavonoids. Photo-sensitizing furocoumarins (psoralens) higher than lemon.
lime peel.cautions: Photo-toxic in concentrated oil/peel contact + sun (phytophotodermatitis blistering). Wash hands after handling.

lime leaf.props: Southeast Asian Magic · Cleansing · Ritual · Bright Air
lime leaf.desc: The makrut lime leaf. Double-lobed. Soul of Thai green curry, tom yum, Cambodian samlor.
lime leaf.descFull: The fragrant double-lobed leaf of the makrut lime tree of Southeast Asia. Used fresh, frozen, or dried. Backbone of Thai green curry, Indonesian rendang, Cambodian samlor. Indispensable; nothing substitutes. The bright lime air of the leaf.
lime leaf.historicUses: Southeast Asian cuisine essential. Indonesian-Cambodian-Thai-Lao cooking. Modern Western fusion adoption.
lime leaf.compounds: Citronellal (dominant aromatic), limonene, sabinene, alpha-pinene. Antimicrobial. Aromatic.
lime leaf.cautions: Generally safe culinary use. Rare contact dermatitis. Tree's thorny fruit not the leaf.

white pepper.props: Hidden Heat · Subtlety · Northern Magic · Pale Fire
white pepper.desc: Black pepper's ripe sibling, husk removed. Subtler heat, distinct earth-funk. White-sauce essential.
white pepper.descFull: Ripe peppercorns soaked and stripped of their outer husk, dried to pale cream. Cleaner heat than black pepper, distinct fermented/earthy note. Pressed into French sauce blanche, Chinese hot-and-sour, Scandinavian pickle. The disappeared pepper.
white pepper.historicUses: Chinese imperial spice. French haute cuisine sauce-pepper. Swedish glogg. Avoids visible specks in cream sauces.
white pepper.compounds: Piperine (slightly lower than black), monoterpenes, less of black's outer-husk aromatics. Digestive stimulant.
white pepper.cautions: Same caveats as black pepper. May potentiate certain drug bioavailability via CYP3A4 inhibition.

green peppercorn.props: Fresh Fire · Bright Subtle · Steak-au-Poivre · French Refinement
green peppercorn.desc: Unripe peppercorns, freeze-dried or brined. Soft, bright, herbaceous. Steak au poivre vert.
green peppercorn.descFull: Unripe peppercorns of the same plant as black and white, harvested green and either freeze-dried or brined. Soft texture, fresh herbaceous heat, less harsh than black. Pressed into French steak au poivre vert, Thai jungle curry (fresh green), Cambodian Kampot.
green peppercorn.historicUses: Modern French haute cuisine essential. Traditional Kampot (Cambodia) fresh-peppercorn cooking.
green peppercorn.compounds: Piperine (lower), monoterpenes, fresh-leaf aromatics. Digestive. Mild antimicrobial.
green peppercorn.cautions: Brined version high in sodium. Otherwise same as black pepper. Generally very safe.

pink peppercorn.props: Sweet Spice · Tropical Berry · Festive Pink · Floral Heat
pink peppercorn.desc: Not a true pepper. Brazilian peppertree berry. Sweet, floral, mild, pretty.
pink peppercorn.descFull: The dried berry of the Brazilian peppertree (not the Piper genus). Pink, papery, sweet-floral with a gentle heat. Pressed into modern French cuisine, Scandinavian cured-salmon mix, pink-pepper bitters. Pretty enough to scatter, mild enough to chew.
pink peppercorn.historicUses: South American traditional spice. Modern Western fashion since the 1980s. Cured salmon (gravlax) classic.
pink peppercorn.compounds: Alpha-pinene, beta-pinene, p-cymene, terpinen-4-ol. Mild antimicrobial. Lower piperine alternative aromatics.
pink peppercorn.cautions: Allergy possible (cashew/mango family). Skin contact may cause irritation in sensitive individuals.

long pepper.props: Ancient Fire · Hindu Magic · Forgotten Spice · Catkin Heat
long pepper.desc: The ancient pepper of Hippocrates. Catkin-shaped, intensely aromatic. Predates black pepper in European trade.
long pepper.descFull: The dried catkin-fruit of an Indian Piper vine. Hotter and more complex than black pepper — smoky, floral, hint of clove. The pepper of antiquity; Hippocrates wrote of it, Romans paid gold for it. Largely forgotten in Europe by 1700, returning now via modern chefs.
long pepper.historicUses: Greek and Roman primary pepper (until black pepper displaced it). Ayurvedic medicine (pippali). Modern revival.
long pepper.compounds: Piperine (higher than black), piperlongumine, pipernonaline. Bioavailability enhancer. Anti-inflammatory.
long pepper.cautions: Higher piperine = stronger CYP3A4 interaction (drug bioavailability). Avoid medicinal doses in pregnancy.

cubeb pepper.props: Indonesian Magic · Pine-Heat · Folk Medicine · Long Spell
cubeb pepper.desc: The tailed pepper of Java. Pine-pepper-bitter. Medieval European pharmacy. Modern Indonesian cuisine.
cubeb pepper.descFull: A stalked peppercorn of Java, with a tiny tail attached. Pine-pepper-bitter, more aromatic than hot. Medieval European apothecaries hoarded it (cubebs were once dearer than black pepper). Indonesian gudeg, gin, bitters, some Moroccan blends.
cubeb pepper.historicUses: Medieval European pharmacy (urinary tonic). Indonesian cuisine. Some gin botanicals. Some Moroccan spice blends.
cubeb pepper.compounds: Cubebin, cubebic acid, alpha-cubebene, sesquiterpenes. Diuretic. Antimicrobial. Bitter digestive.
cubeb pepper.cautions: Bitter and irritating in large amounts. Avoid medicinal doses in pregnancy.

sichuan peppercorn.desc: Not a true pepper. Prickly ash berry. The 'má' of mala — numbing, tingling, citrus-bright.
sichuan peppercorn.descFull: The dried fruit-husk of a Chinese prickly ash tree. Not a Piper, not a Capsicum — a citrus relative. Produces the 'má' (numbing) sensation that pairs with chili 'là' (hot) for Sichuan mala. Mouth-tingle, citrus-floral, bright. Essential in Sichuan and Tibetan cooking.
sichuan peppercorn.historicUses: Pre-modern Chinese imperial spice. Sichuan and Tibetan cooking essential. Modern global tasting-menu darling.
sichuan peppercorn.compounds: Hydroxy-alpha-sanshool (tingling sensation), monoterpenes, geraniol. Mechanoreceptor activation. Mild antimicrobial.
sichuan peppercorn.cautions: Generally very safe. Allergy possible (citrus family). Numbing effect may mask burning sensation; use carefully when hot.

grains of paradise.props: West African Fire · Hidden Spice · Pepper-Substitute · Forgotten Court Magic
grains of paradise.desc: West African seed of the ginger family. Peppery-warm with citrus. Medieval European court-spice.
grains of paradise.descFull: Reddish-brown seeds of a West African herbaceous perennial of the ginger family. Peppery-spicy with citrus and cardamom undertones. Medieval European royal courts paid gold for them; they fell out of fashion as pepper prices dropped. The lost spice of Europe; the live spice of West Africa.
grains of paradise.historicUses: Medieval European court (hippocras, pomanders). West African pepper-soup and yassa. Modern: craft beer, gin, bitters.
grains of paradise.compounds: Paradol, gingerol, shogaol (ginger-family alkaloids), monoterpenes. Anti-inflammatory. Thermogenic. Antioxidant.
grains of paradise.cautions: Generally safe. Avoid medicinal doses in pregnancy. Allergy in ginger-family-sensitive individuals.

juniper berry.desc: The juniper's blue berry. Soul of gin. Strewn on Beltane fires for purification. Pressed into game and choucroute.
juniper berry.descFull: The blue-black berry-like cone of the common juniper, a Northern Hemisphere evergreen. Burned on Beltane and Samhain fires for purification. Soul of gin (genever, juniperus). Pressed into Alsatian choucroute and Scandinavian game. The pine-of-bitter.
juniper berry.historicUses: Beltane purification fire. Plague-house fumigation. Gin and aquavit base. Choucroute essential.
juniper berry.compounds: Alpha-pinene, sabinene, myrcene, terpinen-4-ol, juniper acid. Diuretic. Antimicrobial. Carminative.
juniper berry.cautions: Strong diuretic; avoid in kidney disease and pregnancy. Allergy possible. Old wood and most species except J. communis are toxic.

galangal.props: Southeast Asian Fire · Court Magic · Bright Earth · Pine-Ginger
galangal.desc: Ginger's piney cousin. Heart of Thai tom yum, Indonesian rendang. Sharper, more medicinal than ginger.
galangal.descFull: A rhizome of the ginger family, native to Indonesia and South China. Sharper, more piney, less sweet than ginger. Heart of Thai tom yum and tom kha, Indonesian rendang, Malay rempah. Hildegard of Bingen called it 'the spice of life' in medieval European pharmacy.
galangal.historicUses: Medieval European royal pharmacy ('spice of life'). Southeast Asian curry-paste essential. Lao traditional medicine.
galangal.compounds: 1,8-cineole, alpha-pinene, beta-pinene, galangin (flavonol). Antimicrobial. Anti-inflammatory. Carminative.
galangal.cautions: Generally safe culinary use. May potentiate anticoagulants in medicinal doses. Cross-allergy with ginger family.

horseradish.desc: The white root of sinus-clearing fire. German wedding-table, Passover bitter herb, prime rib classic.
horseradish.descFull: The white tap-root of a brassica, grated raw to release allyl isothiocyanate — the same compound as wasabi, mustard. Sinus-clearing, eye-watering, swift. Passover maror bitter herb. German Sunday-roast condiment. Russian khren. Eastern European Easter-egg dip.
horseradish.historicUses: Passover maror (bitter herb). German classic prepared-horseradish. Russian khren. American cocktail sauce.
horseradish.compounds: Allyl isothiocyanate (formed on grating), sinigrin, vitamin C, glucosinolates. Decongestant. Antimicrobial.
horseradish.cautions: Severe irritation possible with concentrated fresh grate. Eye and sinus warning. Glucosinolates: brassica-allergy caution.

wasabi.props: Japanese Fire · River-Magic · Sushi-Essential · Cold-Water Heat
wasabi.desc: The Japanese green rhizome. Pungent on the breath, fleeting on the tongue. River-grown, expensive, true.
wasabi.descFull: The green rhizome of a Japanese stream-side brassica. Same isothiocyanate family as horseradish and mustard, but a different ratio and a brighter green sweetness. Most 'wasabi' outside Japan is horseradish + green dye. True wasabi is mountain-stream-grown, hand-grated on shark-skin.
wasabi.historicUses: Edo-period sushi essential. Pacific Northwest farming attempts (modern). Cold-mountain-stream cultivation.
wasabi.compounds: Allyl isothiocyanate, 6-methylsulfinyl-hexyl isothiocyanate. Antimicrobial. Decongestant. Anti-platelet.
wasabi.cautions: Real wasabi expensive and rare; commercial 'wasabi' usually horseradish. Anti-platelet caution. Sinus burst harmless.

arrowroot.desc: The starch of the gentle stomach. West Indian rhizome. Thickens sauces without cloud.
arrowroot.descFull: A starchy rhizome of a tropical American herb. Native peoples used it to draw arrow-poison from wounds (the name). Powdered, it thickens sauces with crystal clarity (no cornstarch cloud). Gentle on the stomach. Pressed into Caribbean cooking and infant food.
arrowroot.historicUses: Arawak arrow-poison antidote. Caribbean staple. Victorian infant-food. Modern: gluten-free baking, clear sauce-thickener.
arrowroot.compounds: Pure starch (amylose, amylopectin), trace minerals. No bioactive medicinal compounds in the powder. Gentle.
arrowroot.cautions: Generally very safe (often given to infants). No known interactions. Gluten-free, paleo-friendly.

cassia bark.props: Hearth-Fire · Asian Magic · Stronger Spice · Forest-Bark
cassia bark.desc: The everyday cinnamon. Stronger than Ceylon, coumarin-rich, the spice of American cinnamon-rolls.
cassia bark.descFull: The bark of a Chinese / Vietnamese / Indonesian cassia tree, closer to red-brown and tougher than true Ceylon cinnamon. The 'cinnamon' of most American grocers. Stronger, hotter, higher in coumarin. Pressed into Chinese five-spice, American baking, Vietnamese pho.
cassia bark.historicUses: Chinese five-spice. Vietnamese pho-broth. Mexican champurrado. American cinnamon-roll standard since the 19th century.
cassia bark.compounds: Cinnamaldehyde (75-90%), coumarin (significant), eugenol. Insulin-sensitizing. Anti-inflammatory. Coumarin: hepatotoxic at sustained high intake.
cassia bark.cautions: Coumarin-rich; daily heavy use can stress liver (>6g/day adult). True Ceylon preferred for medicinal use. Avoid in pregnancy in medicinal doses.
cassia bark.pairings: Apple · Brown sugar · Star anise · Pork

annatto.props: Solar Dye · Earth-Color · Caribbean Magic · Hidden-Pepper
annatto.desc: The orange seed of the achiote tree. South American body-paint, Caribbean cooking, the natural orange of cheese.
annatto.descFull: The hard red-orange seed of a tropical American shrub. Crushed by Aztecs and Maya for body paint, war paint, and dye. Pressed into Caribbean and Latin American cooking for color and mild peppery sweetness. The orange in many cheeses (cheddar, Mimolette, Red Leicester) and butter is annatto, not nature.
annatto.historicUses: Aztec / Maya body paint (red). Modern: natural orange food color. Caribbean recaito and sofrito. Yucatan cochinita pibil base.
annatto.compounds: Bixin, norbixin (carotenoid pigments), tocotrienols. Antioxidant. Vitamin A precursor activity. Generally safe.
annatto.cautions: Generally safe culinary use. Rare allergy. Skin / lip staining harmless but persistent.

carom.props: Digestive Fire · Indian Hearth · Earth-Thyme · Indian Magic
carom.desc: Ajwain. Tiny seeds smelling sharply of thyme. South Asian flatbread and pickle essential.
carom.descFull: The small grey-brown seed of a south Indian / Iranian umbellifer. Sharply thyme-scented thanks to thymol content. Indian and Iranian kitchen staple. Pressed into paratha, mathri, savory ajwain biscuits. Folk remedy for digestion across the subcontinent.
carom.historicUses: Ayurvedic digestive (very old). Paratha-dough essential. Iranian / Afghan flatbread. South Asian pickle.
carom.compounds: Thymol (35-60%), gamma-terpinene, p-cymene. Antimicrobial. Carminative. Antispasmodic.
carom.cautions: Strong essential oil; concentrated extracts irritating. Avoid medicinal doses in pregnancy. Lamiaceae-allergy cross-reaction possible.

celery seed.props: Cleansing · Old-Marsh Magic · Diuretic · Roman Crown
celery seed.desc: The tiny seed of celery, more potent than the stalk. Brine-pickle classic, Bloody Mary essential.
celery seed.descFull: The small dark seed of the same plant whose stalks are the kitchen celery. Once a sacred Greek herb (selinon — the wild-celery crown of Nemean Games). Sharper, more savory than the leafy stalk. Pressed into pickling brine, Bloody Mary, coleslaw, celery salt.
celery seed.historicUses: Greek Nemean Games funeral-crown. Roman aphrodisiac. Modern: pickling-spice essential, Bloody Mary, celery salt.
celery seed.compounds: Phthalides (3-n-butyl phthalide), apigenin, limonene, beta-selinene. Diuretic. Mild blood-pressure-lowering.
celery seed.cautions: Avoid medicinal doses in pregnancy (uterine stimulant). Celery allergy can be severe; major allergen. Diuretic effect.

tamarind.props: Sourness · Tropical Magic · Hidden Sweet · Trade-Wind Spice
tamarind.desc: The sour pod of the Indian date. Sour-sweet, fruity, brown. Heart of pad thai, chutney, Worcester.
tamarind.descFull: The brown sticky pulp inside the pod of a tropical leguminous tree. Sour-sweet, fruity, faintly raisin-like. Indian and Southeast Asian kitchen essential. Heart of pad thai, Indian chutney, Worcester sauce, Mexican agua fresca. The trade-wind sour.
tamarind.historicUses: Indian and African staple. Spread by Arab traders. Worcester sauce essential. Latin American agua de tamarindo.
tamarind.compounds: Tartaric acid, citric acid, malic acid, pectin, dietary fiber. Mild laxative. Antioxidant.
tamarind.cautions: High acidity; tooth-enamel caution. Rare allergy. Medicinal laxative effect in large amounts.
tamarind.pairings: Chili · Fish sauce · Brown sugar · Lime

hibiscus.props: Crimson Bloom · Tart Magic · Tropical Sun · Ancestor Drink
hibiscus.desc: The dried crimson sepals of roselle. Tart, ruby-red, vitamin-C rich. Agua de jamaica, Egyptian karkadeh.
hibiscus.descFull: The dried crimson sepals of a tropical mallow, not the petals. Tart, fruity, deeply red. Pressed into Mexican agua de jamaica, Egyptian karkadeh tea, Caribbean sorrel. Hot or iced, cordial or syrup. Sacred to Yoruba weddings; the ancestor drink.
hibiscus.historicUses: Egyptian / Sudanese karkadeh tea. Mexican agua fresca. Yoruba wedding drink. Caribbean Christmas sorrel.
hibiscus.compounds: Anthocyanins, citric and hibiscic acids, vitamin C, polyphenols. Mild antihypertensive. Antioxidant. Diuretic.
hibiscus.cautions: Blood-pressure-lowering; caution if already hypotensive or on antihypertensives. Mild laxative in large amounts.

lemongrass.props: Bright Citrus · Southeast Asian Magic · Cleansing · Tropical Air
lemongrass.desc: The fragrant stalk of citral. Soul of tom yum, satay marinade, Vietnamese pho.
lemongrass.descFull: The pale green-yellow stalk of a tropical grass, lemon-scented from citral. Native to South and Southeast Asia. Sliced or bruised into Thai tom yum, Vietnamese pho-broth, Indonesian satay marinade, Caribbean tea. Bright, clean, herbaceous citrus.
lemongrass.historicUses: Southeast Asian cuisine essential. Traditional Caribbean fevergrass tea. Modern: essential oil aromatherapy.
lemongrass.compounds: Citral (geranial + neral, 65-85%), myrcene, geraniol. Antimicrobial. Antifungal. Mild sedative.
lemongrass.cautions: Generally safe culinary use. Concentrated oil irritating to skin. Cross-allergy with other citrus aromatics.

coconut.props: Abundance · Tropical Hearth · Purity · Lunar Magic
coconut.desc: The tree-of-life palm. Pressed dried flesh, milk, oil, water. Every part feeds.
coconut.descFull: The fruit of the coconut palm — drift-fruit of tropical oceans. Every part edible: water, flesh, milk, oil, husk. Hindu sacred offering. South Indian, Thai, Caribbean, Pacific Islander cuisine essential. Dried desiccated coconut sweetens cake; coconut milk thickens curry. The tree of life.
coconut.historicUses: Pacific Islander staple food. Hindu temple offering (broken at threshold). Modern: vegan dairy alternative.
coconut.compounds: Medium-chain triglycerides (lauric, caprylic acids), cytokinins, vitamins E and K, potassium. Antimicrobial. Cardiovascular-debated.
coconut.cautions: High saturated fat (mostly MCT); cardiovascular impact debated. Allergy uncommon but possible. Coconut water electrolyte balance.

shiitake.props: Umami · Forest Magic · Longevity · Earth-Tree Magic
shiitake.desc: The Japanese log-mushroom. Umami-rich. Pressed into Japanese, Chinese, Korean cooking.
shiitake.descFull: A brown-capped wood-decay mushroom of East Asian oaks and chestnuts. Cultivated on logs for over 1000 years (the word means 'shii oak mushroom'). Heart of Japanese dashi, Chinese stir-fry, Korean banchan. Deep umami, meaty texture. Longevity-tonic in East Asian traditional medicine.
shiitake.historicUses: Chinese / Japanese cuisine 1000+ years. Cultivated on shii (oak) logs. Modern: cancer-adjuvant lentinan derived from shiitake.
shiitake.compounds: Lentinan (beta-glucan polysaccharide), eritadenine, ergothioneine, vitamin D2 (when sun-dried). Immunomodulating. Cholesterol-lowering.
shiitake.cautions: Shiitake dermatitis from raw consumption (flagellate erythema). Always cook well. Allergy possible.

porcini.props: Forest King · Italian Magic · Autumn Hunt · Deep Earth
porcini.desc: The king bolete. Italy's autumn prize. Heart of risotto, porcini pasta, dried-mushroom broth.
porcini.descFull: The thick-stalked tan-capped king bolete of European temperate forests. Italy's porcini ('little pigs'), France's cèpe, Germany's Steinpilz. Hunted in autumn under oak, beech, pine, spruce. Earthy, nutty, faintly forest-floor. Dried, intensified; powder is umami concentrate.
porcini.historicUses: European forest-hunt cuisine since antiquity. Italian autumn ritual food. Modern: dried into umami powder.
porcini.compounds: Ergothioneine, beta-glucans, ergosterol, indole compounds. Antioxidant. Mild immunomodulation.
porcini.cautions: Confirm identification (some bolete cousins are inedible/sickening, none deadly in Europe but caution). Allergy possible.

morel.props: Spring Hunt · Hidden Treasure · Forest Magic · Awakening
morel.desc: The honeycomb-capped spring mushroom. Foraged, never cultivated. Heart of Midwest American spring.
morel.descFull: The honeycomb-pitted hollow-stemmed spring mushroom of temperate northern forests. Foraged in spring after burns, in old apple orchards, under ash and elm. Not commercially cultivated (impossible at scale). Nutty, woodsy, earthy. Heart of Midwest American spring; French haute cuisine morille.
morel.historicUses: Midwest American spring forage tradition. French haute cuisine. Himalayan gucchi (high price). Always foraged, never farmed.
morel.compounds: Indole compounds, glutamic acid, polysaccharides, vitamin D2. Mild antioxidant. Hepatotoxic if raw or undercooked.
morel.cautions: MUST be cooked thoroughly; raw morels are toxic (gyromitrin-like effects). False morel (Gyromitra) deadly; learn the difference.

chanterelle.props: Golden Forest · Apricot-Scent · French Magic · Autumn Joy
chanterelle.desc: The golden funnel of the forest. Apricot-scented, peppery-sweet. European autumn classic.
chanterelle.descFull: The vivid yellow-gold funnel-shaped fruit body of a mycorrhizal mushroom under European and North American conifers and oaks. Apricot-scented, peppery, gently sweet. Cibarius ('for food'). Heart of Polish, German, French, and Russian autumn cuisine. Cannot be confused with deadly look-alikes if approached carefully.
chanterelle.historicUses: Northern European forest-cuisine for centuries. Polish 'kurka,' German 'Pfifferling,' French 'girolle.' Autumn-forage tradition.
chanterelle.compounds: Beta-carotene (gives color), polysaccharides, ergosterol, mannitol. Mild antioxidant.
chanterelle.cautions: False chanterelle (Hygrophoropsis aurantiaca) similar but GI upset only. Jack o' lantern toxic but unmistakable orange.

maitake.props: Dance Mushroom · Immune Magic · Forest Treasure · Japanese Longevity
maitake.desc: Hen-of-the-woods. The 'dance mushroom' of Japan. Found at oak base. Immunomodulating Tonics.
maitake.descFull: A clustered grayish-brown mushroom fruiting at the base of mature oaks in autumn. Japanese 'maitake' means dancing mushroom (foragers danced on finding it). Hen-of-the-woods in English. Earthy, savory. Japanese longevity tonic; pressed into modern immune-support supplements.
maitake.historicUses: Japanese traditional medicine. Japanese cuisine (tempura, sukiyaki). Modern: cancer-adjuvant Maitake D-fraction researched.
maitake.compounds: Beta-glucans (D-fraction, MD-fraction), ergosterol, vitamins D2 and B. Strong immune-modulating. Potential glucose effects.
maitake.cautions: May interact with anti-diabetic medications. Allergy possible. Theoretical anticoagulant effect.

oyster mushroom.props: Wood-Decay Magic · Adaptable · Pearly Bloom · Mycelium-Web
oyster mushroom.desc: The pearly fan of decaying wood. Mild, slightly seafood-suggestive. Easy to cultivate, easy to cook.
oyster mushroom.descFull: A pale gilled mushroom fruiting in shelves on dead and dying hardwood. Native worldwide. Mild flavor faintly suggestive of seafood. Easy to cultivate (paint-bucket, sawdust, straw). Pressed into stir-fry, stroganoff, vegan-meat applications. The friendly oyster.
oyster mushroom.historicUses: Worldwide wild forage. Cultivation popularized in 20th century. WWII Germany cultivated for protein. Modern: home cultivation hobby.
oyster mushroom.compounds: Beta-glucans, lovastatin (natural HMG-CoA inhibitor, low dose), ergothioneine, mannitol. Cholesterol-lowering. Immune-supportive.
oyster mushroom.cautions: Generally very safe. Lovastatin trace; major-dose statin interaction unlikely. Allergy / spore-sensitization possible.

enoki.props: Winter Mushroom · Long Stems · Hot Pot · Snow Magic
enoki.desc: The long pale clustered mushroom of Japanese hot-pot. Cultivated white form most common.
enoki.descFull: A clustered mushroom of decaying hardwood, wild form gold-orange, cultivated form long pale white. Japanese / Korean / Chinese cuisine staple, especially in hot pot, soup, and dumplings. Mild flavor, distinctive crunch. Latin 'flammulina' for the wild form's flame color, 'velutipes' for velvet-foot.
enoki.historicUses: East Asian winter-mushroom (wild form fruits in cold). Cultivated industrially in dark + CO2 to elongate stems. Hot-pot staple.
enoki.compounds: Polysaccharides, ergothioneine, flammulin (protein), vitamin B group. Mild antioxidant. Immune-modulating.
enoki.cautions: Multiple Listeria outbreaks linked to commercial enoki since 2020; cook fully. Allergy possible.
enoki.pairings: Hot pot broth · Soy · Garlic · Pork

portabella.props: Mature Earth · Steak-of-Mushrooms · Common Magic · Grilling-Magic
portabella.desc: The mature brown form of the common button mushroom. Larger, deeper-flavored, more umami.
portabella.descFull: The mature brown form of Agaricus bisporus, same species as white button and cremini. Marketing renamed it 'portabella' in the 1980s to sell mature mushrooms that retailers had discarded. Meaty, deeply earthy, grillable. The vegetarian-burger essential. Cremini is the same species at intermediate maturity.
portabella.historicUses: French 'champignon de Paris' since 1700s. Modern marketing: 1980s name change. Vegetarian-burger applications.
portabella.compounds: Ergosterol (vitamin D2 precursor), B vitamins, selenium, ergothioneine, agaritine (genotoxic in raw form, destroyed by cooking).
portabella.cautions: Raw consumption: agaritine concern (cook thoroughly). Generally very safe cooked. Allergy possible.

wood ear.props: Cloud-Ear · Asian Magic · Forest Ear · Listening
wood ear.desc: The black-brown gelatinous mushroom of dead wood. Soft and chewy. Heart of hot-and-sour soup, banchan.
wood ear.descFull: The dark gelatinous ear-shaped mushroom fruiting on dead deciduous wood, especially elder. Chinese 'mu-er' (wood ear), 'yun-er' (cloud ear). Heart of Chinese hot-and-sour soup, mu shu, Korean japchae. Soft-chewy texture, mild forest flavor. Latin name 'Judas's ear' from old belief he hanged himself on an elder tree.
wood ear.historicUses: Chinese cuisine 2000+ years. Korean japchae component. Medieval European medicinal (eye / throat remedy by signature).
wood ear.compounds: Polysaccharides, dietary fiber, melanin pigments, iron. Mild anticoagulant. Cholesterol-lowering. Soluble fiber.
wood ear.cautions: Mild anticoagulant; caution with blood thinners. Rare allergy. Always cook (raw is unpleasant texture).

ancho chile.props: Mexican Hearth · Dried Sun · Mole Magic · Sweet Smoke
ancho chile.desc: The dried poblano. Heart of mole. Sweet, raisin-dark, mild heat.
ancho chile.descFull: The dried form of the poblano pepper, deep wrinkled mahogany. Sweet, raisin-and-coffee flavored, mild heat (1-2K SHU). Heart of Mexican mole poblano, adobo, chile en nogada. The grandmother chile of Mexican cuisine. Toasted before grinding to wake the flavor.
ancho chile.historicUses: Mexican mole essential. Aztec and modern Mexican kitchen staple. Foundation of red chili pastes and adobos.
ancho chile.compounds: Capsaicin (low-moderate), capsanthin, vitamin C, antioxidants. Anti-inflammatory. Vasodilator.
ancho chile.cautions: Generally safe. Mild heat. Toasting releases capsaicin aerosol; ventilate.

chipotle.props: Smoke · Fire · Mexican Magic · Underworld Heat
chipotle.desc: The smoked jalapeño. Wrinkled mahogany. Heart of adobo, chili con carne, salsa morita.
chipotle.descFull: A jalapeño smoked over wood for days until wrinkled and mahogany. Two main types: chipotle meco (gray-tan, longer-smoked) and morita (red-purple, shorter-smoked). Heart of Mexican adobo sauce and salsa morita. Smoke, fire, depth in dry-pepper form.
chipotle.historicUses: Pre-Columbian Nahua smoke-preservation technique. Mexican adobo. American chipotle-in-adobo canned standard.
chipotle.compounds: Capsaicin (moderate), wood-smoke phenols, capsanthin. Anti-inflammatory. Vasodilator.
chipotle.cautions: Moderate heat. Smoke phenols may irritate sensitive lungs in concentrated handling. Generally safe.

guajillo chile.props: Mexican Sun · Bright Heat · Mole Magic · Berry-Smoke
guajillo chile.desc: The dried mirasol. Smooth red leather. Bright sun-dried heat with berry undertones.
guajillo chile.descFull: The dried form of the mirasol ('looking-at-the-sun') pepper, smooth red leather. Mid-heat (2-5K SHU), tangy with berry and green-tea notes. With ancho and pasilla, one of the holy trinity of Mexican dried chiles. Heart of red mole, salsa roja, birria.
guajillo chile.historicUses: Holy trinity of Mexican mole (with ancho and pasilla). Northern Mexican birria. Tortilla salsa roja.
guajillo chile.cautions: Mid heat. Toasting releases aerosol; ventilate. Generally safe.

pasilla chile.props: Mexican Black · Raisin Heat · Mole Magic · Long-Pepper Shadow
pasilla chile.desc: The dried chilaca. Long black wrinkled. Smoky raisin-cocoa heat. Holy trinity of mole.
pasilla chile.descFull: The dried form of the chilaca pepper, long thin black wrinkled. Often called 'chile negro.' Smoky, raisin-cocoa, mid heat. Third member of the holy trinity (ancho, pasilla, guajillo). Heart of mole negro and Oaxacan cuisine.
pasilla chile.historicUses: Oaxacan mole negro essential. Mexican holy trinity with ancho and guajillo. Mid-century Mexican-American kitchens.
pasilla chile.cautions: Generally safe. Often mislabeled in US stores; verify before buying. Mid heat.

chile de arbol.props: Tree-Chile Fire · Mexican Heat · Bright Burn · Skinny Flame
chile de arbol.desc: The skinny red tree-chile. Sharp clean heat (15-30K SHU). Salsa de chile de arbol classic.
chile de arbol.descFull: A long thin bright-red chile of the cayenne family. Grown on a small tree-like bush (the name means 'tree chile'). Sharp clean heat — straightforward burn without much aromatic complexity. Heart of salsa de chile de arbol, taqueria salsa, hot pepper oil.
chile de arbol.historicUses: Mexican kitchen standard since pre-Columbian. Taqueria salsa essential. Modern: dried-chile oil base.
chile de arbol.cautions: Sharp heat. Eye/skin contact warning. Generally safe.

scotch bonnet.props: Caribbean Fire · Fruit-Heat · Bonnet-Shape · Tropical Sun
scotch bonnet.desc: The Caribbean lantern. 100-350K SHU. Fruity-hot, tropical, soul of jerk and pepper sauce.
scotch bonnet.descFull: A small bonnet-shaped pepper of the Caribbean, close cousin to habanero (same species). Same heat range, slightly sweeter and fruitier. Heart of Jamaican jerk paste, pepper sauce, escovitch, doubles. Bright tropical fire.
scotch bonnet.historicUses: Caribbean staple. Jamaican jerk essential. Trinidadian doubles and pepper sauce. Modern: global hot-sauce inclusion.
scotch bonnet.cautions: High heat. Gloves recommended. GI distress possible in raw consumption. Avoid with reflux.

gochugaru.props: Korean Fire · Sun-Dried · Kimchi Magic · Bright Red Bloom
gochugaru.desc: Korean coarse chili flakes. Sun-dried, deseeded, ground. Mild-moderate heat. Soul of kimchi.
gochugaru.descFull: Korean coarse-ground sun-dried chili flakes, bright red-orange. Mild-moderate heat with a sweet-smoky depth. Heart of kimchi, gochujang base, every Korean soup and stew. Coarser texture than cayenne; deseeded for color rather than burn.
gochugaru.historicUses: Korean cuisine 400+ years (after Capsicum's Columbian-exchange arrival). Kimchi essential. Gochujang base.
gochugaru.compounds: Capsaicin (moderate), carotenoids, vitamin C, sun-dried phenols.
gochugaru.cautions: Mild-moderate heat. Generally safe. Eye contact when handling powder painful.

urfa biber.props: Turkish Dusk · Raisin-Smoke · Slow Burn · Anatolian Magic
urfa biber.desc: The Turkish purple-black chili. Sun-fermented then dried. Slow raisin-smoke heat.
urfa biber.descFull: A Turkish chili (from Urfa, southeastern Anatolia) sun-dried by day and wrapped at night to sweat. Develops dark purple-black color and rich raisin-tobacco-coffee flavor. Mild slow heat. Pressed into Turkish kebab, lamb dishes, ezme.
urfa biber.historicUses: Southeastern Anatolian cuisine. Turkish kebab and meze essential. Modern Western kitchen since ~2010.
urfa biber.compounds: Capsaicin (mild), Maillard products from sweat-drying, dark phenols.
urfa biber.cautions: Mild heat. Generally safe. Salt content varies by producer.

aji amarillo.props: Peruvian Sun · Bright Yellow Fire · Andean Magic · Citrus-Heat
aji amarillo.desc: The Peruvian yellow chile. Bright fruity heat. Heart of aji de gallina, ceviche.
aji amarillo.descFull: A long yellow-orange Andean chile, citrusy-fruity with moderate heat (30-50K SHU). The defining flavor of Peruvian cuisine. Heart of aji de gallina, papa a la huancaina, ceviche-marinade. The Inca's pepper.
aji amarillo.historicUses: Inca staple. Peruvian creole cuisine essential. Modern: global Andean / Nikkei tasting menus.
aji amarillo.compounds: Capsaicin (moderate), capsanthin, fruity esters, vitamin C.
aji amarillo.cautions: Moderate heat. Gloves recommended when seeding. Generally safe.

ghost pepper.props: Searing Fire · Bhut Jolokia · Naga Magic · Ghost-Sweat
ghost pepper.desc: The Indian Bhut Jolokia. 1 million SHU. Fruity-hot beyond reason. Slow brutal burn.
ghost pepper.descFull: An Indian / Bangladeshi pepper of the Naga family. Around 1,000,000 SHU. Fruity-floral aromatics underneath a brutal slow-building burn that peaks 30+ seconds after the bite. Heart of Naga and Assamese cuisine in tiny pinches. Used in tear gas and pepper spray.
ghost pepper.historicUses: Northeast Indian Naga and Bangladeshi cuisine. Modern: hot-sauce industry, eating challenges, riot-control sprays.
ghost pepper.cautions: EXTREME heat. Gloves and goggles required. Severe GI distress. Not for casual cooking. Wash with milk/oil.
ghost pepper.pairings: Fish curry · Mustard oil · Bamboo shoot · Pork

king trumpet.props: Forest-Stem · Vegetarian Magic · Meaty Mushroom · Bell-Tower Fungi
king trumpet.desc: The king oyster. Thick white stem, brown cap. Vegetarian scallop or steak substitute.
king trumpet.descFull: A thick-stemmed oyster-family mushroom of Mediterranean grasslands. The thick stem is the prized portion — meaty, scallop-like when seared, steak-like when slow-cooked. Heart of vegetarian fine dining. Modern cultivation made it commonplace; once a wild-only specialty.
king trumpet.historicUses: Mediterranean wild forage. Modern Italian and Asian commercial cultivation. Vegetarian cuisine signature.
king trumpet.compounds: Beta-glucans, ergothioneine, statins (lovastatin trace), polysaccharides. Mild cholesterol-lowering.
king trumpet.cautions: Generally very safe. Allergy possible. Statin interaction unlikely at culinary doses.

matsutake.props: Japanese Autumn · Pine-Forest · Spice-Aroma · Imperial Treasure
matsutake.desc: The pine mushroom of Japan. Cinnamon-pine perfume. Imperial autumn delicacy. Worth gold per pound.
matsutake.descFull: A white-stemmed brown-capped mycorrhizal mushroom of Japanese red-pine forests. Strong cinnamon-pine-spice perfume. Foraged in autumn, traditionally given as a gift. Rare and expensive — habitat loss + pine wilt disease have decimated Japanese harvests; Pacific Northwest cousins (T. magnivelare) now supply Japan.
matsutake.historicUses: Japanese imperial gift since 8th century. Korean songi. Modern: Pacific Northwest commercial forage for Japan export.
matsutake.cautions: Rare allergy. Confirm identity (look-alikes exist). Always cook.

lobster mushroom.desc: Not a mushroom but a parasite. Bright orange-red. Tastes faintly of shellfish. Fall forage.
lobster mushroom.descFull: A parasitic mold (Hypomyces) that colonizes Russula and Lactarius mushrooms, transforming them into bright orange-red, dense, seafood-flavored fruits. The host gets mummified by the parasite. Fall forage of North American forests. Tastes mildly of lobster — hence the name.
lobster mushroom.historicUses: North American Indigenous and modern forager tradition. Modern fine-dining novelty.
lobster mushroom.compounds: Trans-host carotenoids, parasite compounds. Toxicity of host inherited — generally safe because the parasite attacks safe hosts, but caution.
lobster mushroom.cautions: Theoretically could colonize a toxic host. Forage with experience. Allergy possible. Cook thoroughly.

cauliflower mushroom.desc: The forest-brain mushroom. Looks like brain-coral or noodle bouquet. Found at spruce / pine base.
cauliflower mushroom.descFull: A pale cream / yellow folded ruffled mushroom resembling cauliflower or wrinkled noodle. Grows at the base of conifers in fall. Heart of Japanese and Korean cuisine; revered for texture and adaptogenic properties. Crisp-tender, slightly sweet, holds shape in soup.
cauliflower mushroom.historicUses: East Asian traditional medicine. Modern forage and Korean / Japanese specialty cuisine.
cauliflower mushroom.compounds: Beta-glucans (highly active 1,3 / 1,6 type), polysaccharides, ergosterol. Strong immune-modulating activity.
cauliflower mushroom.cautions: Rare allergy. Cook thoroughly. Some bitterness if older specimens.

lemon balm.desc: The bee's lemon-herb. Calms the nervous heart, lifts the mood. Soul of melissa tea.
lemon balm.descFull: A Lamiaceae perennial smelling sharply of lemon. Greek 'melissa' = honeybee; the plant draws bees. Pressed into Carmelite water (medieval calming tonic), melissa tea, lemon-balm syrup. Calms anxious tummies and racing minds. The gentle nervine.
lemon balm.historicUses: Carmelite water (1611). Persian and Arab nervine tradition. Modern: clinical anxiolytic and Alzheimer's research.
lemon balm.compounds: Citral, citronellal, rosmarinic acid, eugenol. Anxiolytic. GABA-modulating. Mild antiviral (HSV).
lemon balm.cautions: May potentiate sedatives. Hypothyroid caution (lowers TSH historically). Pregnancy: medicinal doses caution.

lemon verbena.props: Aromatic Calm · South American · Dessert-Herb · Bright Air
lemon verbena.desc: The South American lemon-scented shrub. Brighter than lemon balm. Soul of verveine tea, gelato.
lemon verbena.descFull: A spike-leaved South American shrub with intensely lemon-scented foliage. Stronger and brighter than lemon balm. Pressed into French verveine tea, Italian gelato, Argentine lemon-verbena syrup. Calming, digestive, cheerful. Drama-herb of the patio.
lemon verbena.historicUses: Andean traditional herb. French verveine after Empress Eugénie's enthusiasm. Modern artisanal dessert.
lemon verbena.compounds: Citral, geranial, limonene, verbascoside. Antispasmodic. Anxiolytic. Mild antimicrobial.
lemon verbena.cautions: Generally safe. Photosensitization rare. Citrus-allergy cross-reaction possible.

holy basil.props: Sacred Plant · Hindu Devotion · Adaptogen · Heart-Calm
holy basil.desc: Tulsi. The most sacred herb of Hinduism. Planted at every threshold. Adaptogen tea.
holy basil.descFull: A Lamiaceae sacred to Hinduism, worshipped as a manifestation of Lakshmi. Every Hindu home has a tulsi plant. Three main varieties (Rama, Krishna, Vana). Pressed into tulsi tea for stress, immunity, blood-sugar. Distinct clove-anise-mint flavor unlike sweet basil.
holy basil.historicUses: Ayurveda 3000+ years. Hindu daily worship. Modern: clinical adaptogen, stress-modulating research.
holy basil.compounds: Eugenol, ursolic acid, rosmarinic acid, ocimumosides. Adaptogenic. Anti-inflammatory. Blood-sugar modulating. Anxiolytic.
holy basil.cautions: May lower blood sugar; caution with diabetes meds. Antiplatelet (mild). Avoid in pregnancy.

curry leaf.props: South Indian Magic · Aromatic Tempering · Curry-Bright · Tree-of-Spice
curry leaf.desc: The South Indian curry tree leaf. Tempered in hot oil to release perfume. Indispensable.
curry leaf.descFull: The aromatic leaf of a small tree of the Rutaceae family, native to South India and Sri Lanka. Tempered in hot oil/ghee at the start of cooking to release perfume. Indispensable in South Indian cuisine. Distinct from 'curry powder' or curry plant (Helichrysum italicum).
curry leaf.historicUses: South Indian and Sri Lankan cuisine essential. Ayurvedic digestive. Modern global Indian restaurant standard.
curry leaf.compounds: Mahanimbine, koenimbine, beta-caryophyllene, vitamin A. Hypoglycemic. Antioxidant. Hepatoprotective.
curry leaf.cautions: Generally safe culinary. May affect blood sugar — caution with diabetes meds. Allergy rare.

burdock.desc: The deep brown root of the burr-burr plant. Japanese gobo. Earthy, sweet-bitter, blood-cleansing.
burdock.descFull: A long tap-root of a biennial thistle. The burrs inspired Velcro. Japanese gobo, eaten braised or pickled. Traditional Western blood-cleanser and detoxifier — pressed into bitter tonics and ointments. Earthy, faintly sweet, slightly bitter.
burdock.historicUses: Japanese cuisine (kinpira gobo). Western herbalism: blood cleanser, eczema topical. Dandelion-burdock British soda.
burdock.compounds: Inulin (prebiotic), arctigenin, lignans, polyacetylenes, vitamins C, E. Hepatoprotective. Mild antimicrobial. Diuretic.
burdock.cautions: Diuretic. Allergy possible (Asteraceae). Avoid in pregnancy in medicinal doses.

chicory root.props: Coffee-Substitute · Bitter Tonic · French Magic · Endive-Sister
chicory root.desc: The blue-flowered chicory root. Roasted and ground as coffee substitute. New Orleans café au lait.
chicory root.descFull: The tough tap-root of the blue-flowered chicory plant. Roasted and ground, brewed as a coffee substitute that smells of caramel-toffee with bitter edge. Heart of French chicorée and New Orleans café au lait. Rich in inulin — a prebiotic fiber that feeds gut microbiome.
chicory root.historicUses: Roman bitter green. Napoleonic-era coffee extender. American Civil War coffee substitute. New Orleans staple.
chicory root.compounds: Inulin (high), chicoric acid, lactucin, vitamins A, B. Prebiotic. Hepatoprotective. Mild antihyperglycemic.
chicory root.cautions: High inulin may cause bloating / gas. Asteraceae allergy. Bitter principles caution in gallstone disease.

marshmallow root.desc: The soothing demulcent root. Original source of marshmallow confection. Throat balm.
marshmallow root.descFull: The mucilaginous root of a pink-flowered mallow. Pressed into soothing tea and lozenges; original source of the marshmallow confection (the candy is now just sugar / gelatin). The throat-balm — coats and calms inflamed mucous membranes. Western herbal classic.
marshmallow root.historicUses: Egyptian throat lozenge (3000+ years). Medieval European throat / lung tonic. Original marshmallow candy (19th century).
marshmallow root.compounds: Mucilage polysaccharides, flavonoids, asparagine, pectin. Demulcent. Mild anti-inflammatory. Immune-supportive.
marshmallow root.cautions: May slow absorption of other drugs taken concurrently. Generally very safe. Adequate water needed.

ashwagandha.props: Ayurvedic Strength · Adaptogen · Sleep-Tonic · Indian Ginseng
ashwagandha.desc: The Indian ginseng. Ayurvedic adaptogen for stress, sleep, vitality. Earthy, faintly horsey.
ashwagandha.descFull: The dried root of a small Indian Solanaceae. Name means 'smell of horse,' for its earthy musk. Premier Ayurvedic rasayana / rejuvenative. Modern clinical research supports adaptogenic / stress-modulating effects. Pressed into milk or ghee at bedtime traditionally.
ashwagandha.historicUses: Ayurveda 3000+ years (rasayana category). Modern: clinical studies on stress, sleep, athletic performance.
ashwagandha.compounds: Withanolides (withaferin A), withanosides, alkaloids, iron. Adaptogenic. GABA-modulating. Anti-inflammatory.
ashwagandha.cautions: Avoid in pregnancy. May suppress thyroid medication absorption. Solanaceae allergy. May potentiate sedatives.

gotu kola.desc: The brahmi of mountain. Asian creeping herb. Brain-tonic, wound healing, longevity drink.
gotu kola.descFull: A small creeping herb of Asian rice-paddy edges and wet places. Called brahmi alongside Bacopa monnieri (confusion in Western herbalism). Ayurvedic brain-tonic and longevity herb. Modern wound-healing research. Pressed into Indian, Sri Lankan, Indonesian salads and tonics.
gotu kola.historicUses: Ayurvedic medhya rasayana (intellect-tonic). Sri Lankan gotu kola salad. Modern: clinical wound healing.
gotu kola.compounds: Asiaticoside, madecassoside, asiatic acid, triterpene saponins. Wound-healing. Anxiolytic. Vascular-strengthening.
gotu kola.cautions: Hepatotoxicity in rare cases of high-dose long-term use. Pregnancy: avoid. Allergy possible.

maca.props: Andean Strength · Peruvian Ginseng · Fertility · Mountain-Root
maca.desc: The Peruvian high-Andes root. Adaptogen for energy, fertility, mood. Earthy-malty toasted.
maca.descFull: A small turnip-like brassica root of the Peruvian high Andes (4000m+). Earthy, malty, faintly butterscotch toasted. Adaptogen traditionally used by Andean peoples for stamina, fertility, mood. Three color types (yellow / red / black) with slightly different properties.
maca.historicUses: Inca-era staple food and fertility tonic. Modern Western supplement industry since the 1990s.
maca.compounds: Macamides, macaenes, glucosinolates, sterols. Endocrine-modulating (not direct hormone). Energy / mood support.
maca.cautions: Endocrine effects in long-term use — caution with hormone-sensitive conditions. Goitrogenic (raw). Generally safe cooked / powdered.

tonka bean.props: Vanilla-Almond · South American Magic · Forbidden Sweet · Banned-Bean
tonka bean.desc: The Amazon bean of vanilla-almond-cinnamon. Banned by FDA. Loved by chefs. Grated, not eaten whole.
tonka bean.descFull: The dried wrinkled black seed of an Amazonian leguminous tree. Smells of vanilla, almond, cinnamon, hay, tobacco. Coumarin-rich; FDA-banned since 1954 in the US. European fine dining grates it into custards and ice cream where small amounts impart magic. Used like nutmeg.
tonka bean.historicUses: South American traditional perfume bean. European perfumery. Pre-1954 American culinary. Modern French / Belgian fine dining.
tonka bean.compounds: Coumarin (1-3%), benzaldehyde, lactones. Coumarin: hepatotoxic at sustained high doses. Anticoagulant historically.
tonka bean.cautions: US FDA banned for food use. Anticoagulant; avoid with warfarin. Hepatotoxic at high sustained doses. Grate only — never eat whole.

mahleb.props: Levantine Magic · Cherry-Almond · Easter Bread · Hidden Sweet
mahleb.desc: The St Lucie cherry pit's kernel. Cherry-almond bitter perfume. Heart of tsoureki, ma'amoul.
mahleb.descFull: The ground kernel of the St Lucie cherry pit. Bitter-sweet cherry-almond perfume. Heart of Greek Easter tsoureki, Levantine ma'amoul cookies, Turkish breads. Smells like cherry and almond fused. The hidden sweetness of Mediterranean baking.
mahleb.historicUses: Levantine and Greek bread-baking since antiquity. Greek tsoureki essential. Modern Western artisan baking.
mahleb.compounds: Coumarin, amygdalin (cyanogenic), aromatics. Hepatotoxic at sustained high doses. Small amounts (recipe scale) safe.
mahleb.cautions: Amygdalin hydrolyzes to cyanide; small culinary amounts safe. Coumarin caution. Allergy in Rosaceae-sensitive individuals.

pandan leaf.props: Southeast Asian Vanilla · Bright Green · Rice-Bless · Tropical Magic
pandan leaf.desc: The Southeast Asian vanilla. Long blade-shaped leaf. Sweet rice-pandanus perfume. Cooks with rice.
pandan leaf.descFull: The long blade-shaped leaf of a Southeast Asian screwpine. Tied in knots and cooked with rice / coconut milk for sweet vanilla-jasmine-rice flavor. Bright green dye. Heart of Thai khao niao mamuang, Indonesian nasi lemak, Malay pandan cake.
pandan leaf.historicUses: Southeast Asian cuisine essential. Modern: Western Asian-fusion bakery and dessert.
pandan leaf.compounds: 2-Acetyl-1-pyrroline (the popcorn / basmati / pandan smell), beta-carotene, flavonoids.
pandan leaf.cautions: Generally very safe. Rare allergy. Bright green color may stain.

yuzu peel.props: Japanese Citrus · Bright Winter Sun · Imperial Spice · Bath-Magic
yuzu peel.desc: The Japanese yuzu peel. Mandarin-lemon-grapefruit hybrid. Winter solstice bath. Ponzu essential.
yuzu peel.descFull: The aromatic peel of a Japanese hybrid citrus, mandarin x ichang papeda. Brighter and more complex than lemon — mandarin-lemon-grapefruit-pine. Japanese yuzu-yu winter solstice bath custom (whole yuzu floating in the tub). Ponzu base. Modern Western fine-dining favorite.
yuzu peel.historicUses: Japanese cuisine and bath tradition. Korean yuja-cheong (yuzu marmalade). Modern: Western fine-dining citrus.
yuzu peel.compounds: Limonene, gamma-terpinene, hesperidin, vitamin C. Antioxidant. Mood-elevating aroma research.
yuzu peel.cautions: Generally safe. Photo-toxicity unlikely at typical use. Citrus allergy possible.

garam masala.desc: The warming blend of North India. Coriander, cumin, cardamom, cinnamon, clove, pepper. Added at the end.
garam masala.descFull: The hearth-spice of North Indian cooking. Each family holds a slightly different ratio of roasted coriander, cumin, cardamom, cinnamon, clove, black pepper, and bay. Added at the end of cooking to bloom the perfume. The word means 'hot spice' (warm to the body, not the tongue).
garam masala.historicUses: Mughal court cuisine. Tandoori marinade. Curry-finishing spice. Modern: global Indian restaurant standard.
garam masala.compounds: Mixed essential oils from constituent spices. Carminative. Anti-inflammatory.
garam masala.cautions: Generally safe. Allergies possible to constituent spices.

ras el hanout.props: Maghreb Magic · Royal Mix · Souk-Splendor · Earth-Sun
ras el hanout.desc: 'Top of the shop' — the spice merchant's finest. Can hold 20-40 spices. Soul of tagine.
ras el hanout.descFull: The Maghreb's pride. Literally 'head of the shop'; the spice merchant's most prized blend. Each family or vendor guards their proportions of cardamom, mace, nutmeg, anise, galangal, rose petal, lavender, cumin, clove, and more. Soul of tagine and couscous.
ras el hanout.historicUses: Moroccan and Tunisian royal kitchen. Modern: global tagine cuisine, occasional French haute-cuisine borrowing.
ras el hanout.compounds: Mixed essential oils, eugenol, rose volatiles, cardamom cineole. Anti-inflammatory.
ras el hanout.cautions: Some traditional recipes contain Spanish fly or other unsafe ingredients; verify source. Allergies to constituents.

baharat.props: Levantine Warmth · Coffee-Bowl Magic · Lamb-Spice · Hearth
baharat.desc: The 'spice' of the Levant. Black pepper, allspice, cinnamon, clove, cardamom, paprika, nutmeg. Lamb's friend.
baharat.descFull: The general-purpose Levantine warm-spice blend. The Arabic word literally just means 'spices.' Black pepper, allspice, cinnamon, clove, cardamom, paprika, nutmeg. Levantine kitchens keep a coffee-grinder full of it for kebabs, rice pilafs, lentil soup.
baharat.historicUses: Levantine and Persian Gulf kitchen tradition. Modern: global Levantine restaurant standard.
baharat.compounds: Mixed warm-spice essential oils, eugenol, cinnamaldehyde. Carminative.
baharat.cautions: Allergy possible to any constituent spice.

berbere.props: Ethiopian Fire · Stew-Heart · Highland Magic · Berbere-Sun
berbere.desc: The fire of Ethiopia and Eritrea. Chili, fenugreek, ginger, korarima, ajwain. Heart of doro wat, key wat.
berbere.descFull: The defining spice of Ethiopian and Eritrean cooking. Chili (lots), fenugreek, ginger, garlic powder, korarima (false cardamom), ajwain, cardamom, coriander, nigella, cinnamon, clove. Toasted, ground, sun-dried. Soul of doro wat, key wat, misir wat.
berbere.historicUses: Ethiopian highland cuisine. Modern: global Ethiopian restaurant standard.
berbere.compounds: Capsaicin (high), eugenol, gingerol, terpenes. Anti-inflammatory. Thermogenic.
berbere.cautions: High heat. Niter kibbeh (Ethiopian spiced butter) often paired. Allergy possible.

cajun seasoning.desc: Louisiana's spice. Paprika, cayenne, garlic, onion, oregano, thyme, pepper. Soul of jambalaya, blackening.
cajun seasoning.descFull: The defining blend of Cajun Louisiana cooking. Paprika, cayenne, garlic powder, onion powder, oregano, thyme, black and white pepper. Soul of jambalaya, gumbo, blackened fish, dirty rice. Working-kitchen blend; every cook has their proportions.
cajun seasoning.historicUses: Louisiana Acadian (Cajun) tradition since 18th century. Modern: blackening technique (Paul Prudhomme, 1980s).
cajun seasoning.compounds: Capsaicin (moderate), allium sulfides, paprika carotenoids, thymol.
cajun seasoning.cautions: Moderate heat. Salt content varies wildly by brand. Allium-allergy possible.

chili powder.props: Tex-Mex Hearth · Bowl-of-Red · Cumin-Earth · Working Kitchen
chili powder.desc: The American Southwest blend. Dried chiles, cumin, oregano, garlic, paprika. Soul of chili con carne.
chili powder.descFull: Not just ground chile but a blend — dried mild chiles (ancho most common), cumin, Mexican oregano, garlic, paprika, sometimes salt. American invention. Defines chili con carne, taco filling, enchilada sauce. Distinct from pure ground chile.
chili powder.historicUses: Created in 1890s San Antonio by William Gebhardt. American Southwest staple ever since.
chili powder.compounds: Mixed capsaicin, cumin aldehyde, oregano terpenes, allium sulfides.
chili powder.cautions: Mild-moderate heat. Sodium can be high in pre-blended brands.

five spice.props: Five Elements · Imperial Magic · Roast Meat · Yin-Yang
five spice.desc: Chinese imperial blend. Star anise, fennel, cinnamon, clove, Sichuan peppercorn. Five flavors balanced.
five spice.descFull: The Chinese imperial five-spice. Star anise, fennel, cinnamon (cassia), clove, Sichuan peppercorn. Balances the five flavors (sweet, sour, bitter, salty, umami) and the five elements. Soul of roast duck, char siu pork, red-braised dishes. Traditional Chinese medicine framework.
five spice.historicUses: Chinese imperial kitchen since at least Tang dynasty. Modern: ubiquitous in Cantonese, Hunan, Sichuan cooking.
five spice.compounds: Anethole, eugenol, cinnamaldehyde, sanshool. Carminative. Anti-inflammatory.
five spice.cautions: Coumarin from cassia at heavy use. Sichuan sanshool numbing.

harissa.props: Maghreb Fire · Caraway-Heat · Tunisian Magic · Sun-Pasted
harissa.desc: The Maghreb's chili paste. Smoked chiles, caraway, coriander, garlic, olive oil. Tunisian core.
harissa.descFull: The defining hot paste of Tunisia, Algeria, Libya, Morocco. Roasted dried red chiles ground with caraway, coriander, cumin, garlic, salt, olive oil. Sun-dried, jarred. Spread on bread, stirred into couscous, marinated onto lamb. Smoke and sun and seed.
harissa.historicUses: Maghreb traditional paste. Modern: global Mediterranean and fusion adoption.
harissa.compounds: Capsaicin, caraway carvone, cumin aldehyde, allium sulfides. Anti-inflammatory.
harissa.cautions: Moderate heat. Salt level varies. Allergy to constituents.

herbes de provence.props: Lavender Hill · Mediterranean Sun · Provençal Magic · Bouquet-Memory
herbes de provence.desc: The blend of southern France. Thyme, rosemary, savory, oregano, marjoram, sometimes lavender.
herbes de provence.descFull: The dried-herb blend of Provence. Thyme, rosemary, savory, oregano, marjoram, basil, fennel seed, sometimes (controversially) lavender. American versions tend to include lavender; French traditionalists skip it. Soul of grilled fish, roast lamb, ratatouille.
herbes de provence.historicUses: Provençal cooking tradition. Modern (post-1970s): packaged blend exported globally.
herbes de provence.compounds: Thymol, rosmarinic acid, carvacrol, linalool. Antimicrobial. Mild anti-inflammatory.
herbes de provence.pairings: Olive oil · Lamb · Tomato · Goat cheese

italian seasoning.desc: The American-Italian blend. Oregano, basil, thyme, rosemary, marjoram, sage. Pizza-pasta default.
italian seasoning.descFull: A largely American invention reflecting Italian-American kitchen needs. Oregano, basil, thyme, rosemary, marjoram, sage. Not used in Italy proper (Italians reach for individual herbs). Soul of red sauce, meatballs, pizza, garlic bread. The pantry-friendly default.
italian seasoning.historicUses: Italian-American mid-20th-century convenience invention. Modern: ubiquitous suburban-pantry blend.
italian seasoning.compounds: Carvacrol, thymol, linalool, rosmarinic acid. Antimicrobial.

jerk seasoning.props: Island Fire · Allspice-Smoke · Scotch Bonnet · Pimento-Wood
jerk seasoning.desc: The Jamaican blend. Allspice, scotch bonnet, thyme, scallion, ginger, cinnamon. Smoke and fire.
jerk seasoning.descFull: The defining blend of Jamaican jerk cooking. Allspice (pimento), scotch bonnet, thyme, scallion, ginger, cinnamon, clove, nutmeg. Wet (paste) or dry (rub) versions both exist. Traditionally cooked over pimento-wood smoke. Maroon origin — Jamaican Maroons developed the technique escaping slavery.
jerk seasoning.historicUses: Jamaican Maroon tradition since 17th century. Modern: global Jamaican restaurant signature.
jerk seasoning.cautions: Hot. Allspice-eugenol caution at heavy use.

mole spice.props: Oaxacan Magic · Chocolate-Mole · Ceremonial · Deep Earth
mole spice.desc: The dry foundation of Oaxacan mole. Ancho, pasilla, guajillo, chocolate, sesame, cinnamon, clove.
mole spice.descFull: The dried-spice foundation of Mexican mole poblano and mole negro. Ancho, pasilla, guajillo, mulato chiles + cocoa, sesame, cinnamon, clove, anise, cumin, almond. Ground together, fried in lard, simmered into the sauce. Oaxacan ceremonial food; weddings, holidays, life-events.
mole spice.historicUses: Pre-Columbian Aztec roots. Spanish chocolate fusion (16th c). Oaxacan ceremonial food.
mole spice.compounds: Theobromine (cacao), capsaicin (mild), eugenol, cinnamaldehyde. Mixed bioactives.

mulling spice.props: Yule Hearth · Wassail Magic · Winter Warmth · Solstice
mulling spice.desc: The wassail blend. Cinnamon stick, clove, allspice, orange peel, star anise. Pressed into hot cider, wine.
mulling spice.descFull: The English / European winter-warming blend. Cinnamon sticks, whole cloves, allspice berries, orange peel, star anise, cardamom, sometimes black pepper. Tied in cheesecloth or steeped loose in hot cider, wine, brandy, or rum. Yule fireside essential. The smell of the season.
mulling spice.historicUses: Medieval English mulled wine (ypocras). Scandinavian glögg. Caribbean and American wassail. Yule tradition.
mulling spice.compounds: Cinnamaldehyde, eugenol, anethole, limonene. Carminative. Anti-inflammatory.
mulling spice.cautions: Coumarin (cassia) with heavy use. Allergy possible.

panch phoron.desc: Bengali five-seed temper. Fenugreek, nigella, cumin, fennel, mustard. Whole seeds, hot oil.
panch phoron.descFull: The five-seed tempering blend of Bengal and Bihar. Equal parts fenugreek, nigella, cumin, fennel, mustard or radhuni. Always whole, never ground. Crackled in hot ghee or mustard oil at the start of cooking to bloom. Soul of Bengali fish curry, dal, vegetable bhaja.
panch phoron.historicUses: Bengali traditional tempering blend. Bihari and Odia regional. Modern: global Bengali restaurant standard.
panch phoron.compounds: Mustard isothiocyanate, anethole, nigella thymoquinone, fenugreek galactomannan.

pickling spice.props: Brine Magic · Pantry Defense · Vinegar-Earth · Working Kitchen
pickling spice.desc: The English pickling blend. Mustard, coriander, bay, allspice, clove, peppercorn, dill, chili.
pickling spice.descFull: The English-tradition pickling blend. Mustard seed, coriander seed, bay leaf, allspice, clove, black peppercorn, dill seed, cinnamon, chili flake. Steeped in brine or vinegar to make pickles, chutneys, corned beef brine. Pantry-defense magic.
pickling spice.historicUses: English pickling tradition since at least 17th century. Modern: corned-beef brine, bread-and-butter pickles.
pickling spice.compounds: Mustard isothiocyanate, coriander linalool, eugenol, piperine. Antimicrobial. Carminative.
pickling spice.cautions: Mustard-allergy caveat. Bay leaves choking hazard if not strained.

poultry seasoning.props: Thanksgiving Hearth · Sage-Memory · Family-Magic · Roast Bird
poultry seasoning.desc: The American Thanksgiving blend. Sage, thyme, marjoram, rosemary, savory, pepper. Stuffing's soul.
poultry seasoning.descFull: The American holiday-meal blend. Heavy sage with thyme, marjoram, rosemary, savory, nutmeg, white pepper. Soul of Thanksgiving stuffing, roast chicken, gravy. Mid-19th-century American invention; tied to colonial English sage-and-onion traditions.
poultry seasoning.historicUses: American colonial sage-stuffing tradition. Bell's Seasoning (1867, William Bell) the famous commercial brand.
poultry seasoning.compounds: Thujone (sage), thymol, rosmarinic acid, eugenol. Antimicrobial.
poultry seasoning.cautions: High-thujone sage with heavy use is mildly neurotoxic. Culinary amounts safe.

pumpkin pie spice.props: Autumn Hearth · Pumpkin-Magic · Yule Approach · Hearth-Cinnamon
pumpkin pie spice.desc: The American autumn dessert blend. Cinnamon-heavy with ginger, nutmeg, clove, allspice.
pumpkin pie spice.descFull: The American autumn / Thanksgiving dessert blend. Cinnamon-dominant, with ginger, nutmeg, clove, allspice. Soul of pumpkin pie, spiced latte, pumpkin bread, autumn cookies. Pre-blended commercially since the 1930s; before that, cooks blended each time.
pumpkin pie spice.historicUses: Colonial American pumpkin pie tradition. McCormick Pumpkin Pie Spice (1934) commercial standard. Modern: pumpkin spice latte cultural phenomenon.
pumpkin pie spice.cautions: Coumarin (cassia) with heavy use. Myristicin (nutmeg) high-dose toxicity. Culinary safe.

shichimi togarashi.props: Seven Flavors · Japanese Magic · Bowl-Top · Mountain Pepper
shichimi togarashi.desc: The Japanese seven-flavor blend. Chili, sansho, sesame, nori, citrus peel, hemp seed, poppy.
shichimi togarashi.descFull: The Japanese seven-flavor mountain-chili blend. Red chili, sansho (mountain pepper), white sesame, black sesame, nori (or aonori), dried orange peel, hemp seed (or poppy seed), ginger. Sprinkled on udon, soba, donburi, gyudon. Each shop has its proportions; Kyoto and Tokyo differ.
shichimi togarashi.historicUses: Edo-period Japanese street food. Modern: Japanese restaurant table-condiment global standard.
shichimi togarashi.compounds: Capsaicin (low-moderate), sanshool (numbing), sesame lignans, citrus limonene.
shichimi togarashi.cautions: Sanshool numbing. Sesame allergy. Generally very safe.

taco seasoning.props: Tex-Mex Hearth · Weeknight Magic · Family-Friendly · Working Kitchen
taco seasoning.desc: The Tex-Mex weeknight blend. Chili powder, cumin, garlic, onion, oregano, paprika.
taco seasoning.descFull: The American Tex-Mex weeknight blend. Chili powder (which is itself a blend), cumin, garlic powder, onion powder, oregano, paprika, sometimes flour as thickener. Soul of ground-beef taco night. Distinct from Mexican mole and authentic salsas — its own thing.
taco seasoning.historicUses: Mid-20th-century Tex-Mex commercial invention. Lawry's, McCormick, Old El Paso brand standardizations.
taco seasoning.compounds: Capsaicin (mild), cumin aldehyde, allium sulfides, paprika carotenoids.
taco seasoning.cautions: Sodium content high in commercial blends. Mild heat.

tandoori spice.props: Tandoor Fire · Punjab Magic · Clay-Oven Roast · Yogurt-Cool
tandoori spice.desc: The clay-oven roasting blend. Cayenne, paprika, garam masala, ginger, garlic, fenugreek. Annatto color.
tandoori spice.descFull: The marinade-spice for Punjabi tandoor cooking. Cayenne, paprika, garam masala, ginger, garlic, fenugreek leaf (kasoori methi), ground coriander, cumin, turmeric. Often colored with annatto or beet for the classic red. Marinated into yogurt and rubbed onto chicken before the clay oven.
tandoori spice.historicUses: Punjabi tandoor tradition (centuries). Modern: global Indian restaurant tandoori chicken.
tandoori spice.compounds: Capsaicin (moderate), paprika carotenoids, fenugreek sotolon, turmeric curcumin.

za'atar.props: Levantine Magic · Olive-Bread · Hearth-Memory · Mountain Herb
za'atar.desc: The Levantine blend. Wild thyme, sumac, sesame, salt. Sprinkled on labneh, bread, oil.
za'atar.descFull: The defining Levantine herb-seed blend. Wild Levantine thyme (za'atar) — a Lamiaceae distinct from European thyme — plus sumac, toasted sesame, salt. Sometimes oregano, marjoram, hyssop blended in. Sprinkled on labneh and olive oil, on man'oushe bread, on roast vegetables. Memory-food across Levant.
za'atar.historicUses: Levantine kitchen since antiquity. Modern: global Levantine restaurant signature.
za'atar.compounds: Thymol, carvacrol, sumac tannins, sesame lignans.
za'atar.cautions: Sesame allergy. Wild za'atar (Origanum syriacum) is protected in some regions due to over-harvesting.

vadouvan.props: Pondicherry Magic · French-Indian · Roasted Curry · Bridge-Spice
vadouvan.desc: French-Indian roasted curry blend. Onion-shallot-garlic dried + cumin, fenugreek, mustard, curry leaf.
vadouvan.descFull: A blend that bridges French and Pondicherry-Tamil cuisine. Onion, shallot, garlic dried and ground with cumin, fenugreek, mustard, curry leaf, fennel, sometimes cardamom. Roasted before grinding. Heart of modern French-Indian fusion. The European curry that French cooks actually want to use.
vadouvan.historicUses: French Pondicherry colonial cuisine. Modern: French Laundry / California cuisine adoption.
vadouvan.compounds: Allium sulfides (dried alliums), fenugreek sotolon, cumin aldehyde, curry-leaf mahanimbine.
